summary: The dramatic arc, character differentiation, continuity, and willingness to confront its premise's darkest implications are genuinely strong — several set pieces (the demolition, the email discovery, the Sarah confrontation, the Chapter 5 sex scene) approach award caliber, and cross-chapter continuity across all eighteen parallel-drafted chapters is flawless. Five craft-level failure modes prevent shortlist readiness, listed here in order of consequence. (1) Overstatement after arrival: the novel's most persistent structural weakness. Scenes consistently land their meaning through concrete action, then continue for 5–20 lines of abstract restatement, moral paraphrase, or thesis language that converts dramatic arrivals into explanatory essays. This affects at least six chapters and undermines reader trust that the prose will let its images work. (2) Prose-formula saturation: three rhetorical constructions — 'not because X but because Y' (37 instances across 16 of 18 chapters), 'the kind of [noun] that' (29 instances across 14 chapters), and the triple-suppression dialogue move 'almost said X; instead said Y' (20+ instances across 11 chapters) — recur with enough density to create a detectable generative texture that a major-award reader would register by midbook. (3) Victor's dialogue as thesis prose: in his sustained scenes (Chapters 14 and 16 especially), Victor delivers essay-grade compressed insights that read as authorial argument rather than spoken language under pressure, risking his conversion from character to wisdom-delivery system. (4) Style-bible example-line leakage: three lines explicitly marked 'CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION' appear verbatim in the manuscript (Chapters 6, 10, 15). A clear protocol breach. (5) Narrator relationship to dialogue: the word 'landed' describes how dialogue affects Nate fifteen times across the novel, creating a narrator-level tic; three passages explicitly admire how well a character's line worked, converting overheard speech into reviewed performance. A secondary concern: despite a fully rendered Chapter 5 sex scene, the novel's physical-intimacy dimension disappears entirely for the remaining thirteen chapters, defaulting to domestic gesture and euphemistic shorthand in a premise that specifically demands bodily reckoning with consciousness replacement and sexual fraud. All issues are fixable at the prose surface without structural change. The underlying arc, character differentiation, emotional engine, and moral complexity are sound.

findings.problem: Style-bible example-line leakage. Renata's dialogue '"You seem different," she said. "Good different."' at line 2404 is a verbatim reproduction of the style-bible calibration line for renata_altieri: 'CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: "You seem different. Good different." [to Dominic/Nate at the door, simple observation with enormous weight]'. The calibration label explicitly prohibits reproduction in the manuscript.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the line at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2404 with a Renata observation that conveys the same dramatic beat — she notices a change in her son — without reproducing the calibration text. Options: (a) Renata comments on a specific behavioral change she noticed during dinner ('You listened tonight. I mean really listened. That's new.'); (b) Renata makes a physical observation ('You look rested. You never look rested.'); (c) Renata says something oblique that reveals the observation through her characteristic indirectness ('Whatever she's feeding you, keep eating it.'). The replacement must sound like Renata — short, declarative, delivered while already moving to the next task. Preserve the paragraph at lines 2405–2414 that processes Nate's reaction; adjust its internal references to match the new line.
findings.acceptance_test: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2404, the revised line must: (1) not contain the phrases 'seem different' or 'good different' in any form; (2) convey Renata's perception that Dominic has changed; (3) read as natural Renata-register speech (short, direct, affectionate-through-bluntness); (4) not contain any other style-bible calibration phrase.

findings.problem: Style-bible example-line leakage. Ray's dialogue '"You didn't know who that was"' at lines 4177 and 4181 is a verbatim reproduction of the style-bible calibration line for ray_dunlap: 'CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: "You didn't know who that was." [not a question — a statement, after the client call]'. The line appears twice in the same exchange, doubling the exposure. The calibration label explicitly prohibits reproduction.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace Ray's lines at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4177 and 4181. Ray's challenge must still convey that Nate failed to recognize a key client, but in different words. Options: (a) Ray states the specific consequence ('That was Halpern. Your file. Your client. And you looked at him like a stranger.'); (b) Ray makes it personal ('You blanked on your own client, man.'); (c) Ray asks a question that is really an accusation ('How do you not know Halpern?'). At line 4181, the repeated version should be rewritten as a follow-up that escalates rather than restates — e.g., Ray naming what the failure means for Nate's position.
findings.acceptance_test: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4177 and 4181, the revised lines must: (1) not contain the phrase 'didn't know who that was' in any form; (2) preserve Ray's directness and the dramatic function (confronting Nate's professional incompetence); (3) sound like Ray's established register (short, practical, slightly profane); (4) the two instances must use different phrasing from each other — no verbatim self-repetition.

findings.problem: Style-bible example-line leakage. Tommy's dialogue '"Thanks, Dom," he said.' at line 6441 is a verbatim reproduction of the style-bible calibration line for tommy_altieri: 'CALIBRATION ONLY — NOT FOR REPRODUCTION: "Thanks, Dom." [quiet, after the money, the charm gone, just a brother]'. The manuscript deploys it in the exact same dramatic context described in the calibration note (quiet, after receiving money, the charm dropped). The calibration label explicitly prohibits reproduction.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace Tommy's line at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:6441. The beat — Tommy's charm falling away, leaving genuine gratitude or shame — must be preserved in different words or through action rather than speech. Options: (a) Tommy says nothing; the beat is carried entirely by the physical action at the door (the hug is already there); (b) Tommy says something that sounds like him but is not the calibration phrase ('I'll pay you back' — delivered flat, without his usual salesmanship, so the reader knows he knows he won't); (c) Tommy starts to say something performative, catches himself, and says something smaller ('I — yeah.'); (d) Tommy says the brother's name instead: 'Dom,' and nothing else, as a standalone vocative that carries everything.
findings.acceptance_test: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:6441, the revised line must: (1) not contain the phrase 'Thanks, Dom' in any form; (2) convey Tommy's stripped-down emotional state (charm gone, genuine feeling exposed); (3) match Tommy's established voice (even at his most vulnerable, he is not articulate — he is either performing or collapsing, not balanced); (4) not exceed two short sentences.

findings.problem: Overstatement after arrival. The chapter's devastating payload arrives at the email chain showing Dominic fought the demolition (lines 4459-4515). The evidence is self-explanatory. But the chapter then spends approximately 90 additional lines (4517-4605) restating, re-reading, and abstractly summarizing what the emails mean. Lines 4539-4555 are the heaviest offenders: 'He had not just killed the wrong man. Even that came out too neat... Dominic had gone first. Dominic had gone better.' While individual sentences are strong, the cumulative effect is the narrator doing the reader's work. Lines 4591-4597 employ rhetorical stacking for gravitas ('There had been a letter. / There had been a signature. / There had also been this.') that tips from earned restraint into composed crescendo. The final line at 4605 ('He had killed a man who was trying to help') is powerful but lands after the reader has already assembled that conclusion three different ways.
findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the post-discovery section at snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4517-4605 by approximately 50-60%. Preserve: (a) Nate's immediate physical reaction (the paragraph near 4515-4520); (b) one key interior sentence that articulates the specific new knowledge (Dominic fought first, Dominic lost); (c) the chapter's closing line or a variation of it. Cut: (d) the 'not just killed the wrong man / even that came out too neat' editorial sequence at 4539-4555 — the reader does not need the narrator to grade the irony; (e) the tripled 'There had been...' crescendo at 4591-4597; (f) the passages that re-read or re-summarize the email content the reader already absorbed. The chapter should end within 15-20 lines of the last email, not 90.
findings.acceptance_test: The post-email-discovery section (everything after the final email quote) must: (1) not exceed 35 lines; (2) contain no more than one sentence that abstractly names Dominic's moral position (currently there are at least four); (3) not re-paraphrase email content the reader already read; (4) end on a concrete image, sensation, or single declarative — not a rhetorical triple. Read the revised passage: if the narrator is telling the reader what to feel about the emails rather than letting the emails speak, the passage fails.

findings.problem: Post-confrontation sprawl. After Sarah moves to the couch (line 5034) and the apartment goes silent, the chapter continues for approximately 175 lines. The confession-rehearsal passages (5112-5141) are dramatically strong. But lines 5144-5208 cover ground the confrontation already established: Nate knows he should confess, cannot, and will not. The phrase 'It was fear, plain and middle-sized and complete' (line 5202) is fine in isolation but arrives after the point has been made through the preceding scene action. The chapter's tail restates the same emotional territory — fear of exposure, awareness of fraud — that Sarah's kitchen scene already communicated through dialogue and gesture.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:5144-5208, cut 40-60 lines. Preserve the specific sensory details of lying in the dark (couch springs, apartment sounds, the imagined cushion seam at her hip). Cut the recursive internal loops that restate the confrontation's already-delivered meaning. In particular: lines where Nate re-summarizes what Sarah said, lines where the narration explains that he is afraid (the scene has shown this), and lines where he processes the moral architecture of his position in abstract terms. The chapter should move from the confession-rehearsal (which is strong) to the decision to stay in bed (which is the chapter's real ending) in roughly 30 lines, not 65.
findings.acceptance_test: The post-couch section (lines 5034 onward) must: (1) not exceed 120 lines total (currently ~175); (2) contain no abstract summary of what Sarah's confrontation 'meant'; (3) end with a concrete image or action, not a thesis statement about fear or fraud; (4) the confession-rehearsal at lines 5112-5141 should be preserved but the lines after it should move toward the chapter's end without restating its payload.

findings.problem: Overstatement after Victor's devastating verdict. Victor's final assessment of Dominic at lines 5998-6003 ('Because he was good. Because he could make the humane case better than anybody in this office...') is the scene's natural climax. But lines 6010-6028 then reprocess the verdict in interior narration: 'The trap was not abstract anymore' and the drive to Eastbridge recapitulates Victor's argument in reflective summary rather than new action. The reader has already understood what Victor's speech means for Nate; the narrator's re-explanation diminishes Victor's authority by translating his impact into Nate's simpler terms.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:6010-6028, cut the interior reflection to 5-8 lines. Preserve the transition to the car and the drive to Eastbridge (the image of Nate watching Mort through the hardware store window is earned). Cut the narrator's interpretation of what Victor's verdict means — let the preceding dialogue carry its own weight. Specifically, remove or compress any sentence at lines 6012-6018 that restates Victor's argument in Nate's interior voice.
findings.acceptance_test: Lines 6010-6028 must: (1) not contain narrator-voice restatement of Victor's argument; (2) transition to the Eastbridge drive in 5-8 lines or fewer; (3) end the chapter on the concrete image (Mort in the store, or equivalent) rather than on an abstract realization.

findings.problem: Overstatement after signing. The signing scene at lines 7084-7091 is rendered with excellent physical precision (pen, ink, paper fibers). The anti-climax at line 7094 ('No wave of nausea. No courtroom in his head') works. But lines 7098-7102 then convert the signing into thesis language: 'This was the point of contact. This was where the resemblance stopped being costume and became choice.' And 'Given the same walls, the same money, the same boss... he had arrived at the same line and done the same thing.' The scene has already communicated this through the act of signing; the narrator's explanation is redundant.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:7094-7102, cut the thesis sentences at lines 7098-7102. Keep the anti-climax beat (7094) and move directly to the physical act of carrying the packet to Victor's inbox. The signing's meaning — that Nate has become what he killed Dominic for being — is self-evident from the scene action. It does not need to be stated as 'the resemblance stopped being costume and became choice.'
findings.acceptance_test: Lines 7094-7102 must: (1) not contain the phrases 'point of contact,' 'costume and became choice,' or any equivalent abstract thesis about what the signing means; (2) move from the signing to the inbox delivery in 4-5 lines; (3) carry the chapter's meaning through action and physical detail, not narrator commentary.

findings.problem: Two related issues in the post-demolition section. (1) Overstatement: lines 7313-7315 restate the chapter's thematic point in explicit philosophical terms ('He had come back to the hinge and found the same door') after the demolition scene has already communicated this through action. Line 7373 ('He tried, once, to think in the big moral terms... The thoughts came up flat, useless, pre-chewed') is the narrator telling us it is not going to philosophize and then philosophizing about why it is not philosophizing. (2) 'As if' density: the demolition chapter uses 'as if' constructions twelve times in 308 lines — triple the book average — reflecting the narration straining to process the demolition through simile rather than direct statement. Both problems stem from the same root: the narrator reaching for interpretive frames when the physical details are already doing the work.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:7313-7381: (a) Cut the 'hinge/door' metaphor at 7313-7315 and the 'tried to think in big moral terms' passage at 7373 — these are the narrator explaining what the reader has already felt. (b) Across the full chapter (lines 7105-7412), reduce 'as if' constructions from 12 to no more than 4. Prioritize keeping the 3-4 strongest similes and replacing the rest with direct statement or concrete sensory description. The demolition is powerful enough to be rendered through what Nate sees, hears, and physically feels without requiring constant analogical interpretation.
findings.acceptance_test: The revised chapter must: (1) contain no more than 4 'as if' constructions total; (2) not contain abstract thesis statements about what the demolition means for Nate's moral position; (3) end its post-demolition section within 40 lines of the last physical demolition action, with the screw-in-rubble and drive home carrying the chapter's meaning through object and action.

findings.problem: Prose-formula density. Chapter 4 contains five instances of 'the kind of [noun] that' (lines 1128, 1320, 1368 [doubled in one paragraph], and two more nearby) and three instances of 'not because X but because Y' within 545 lines. Both constructions do identical rhetorical work — they block the reader's assumed interpretation and substitute a more precise one — and their co-occurrence within a single chapter creates a detectable explanatory rhythm. 'The kind of' appears 29 times across 14 chapters novel-wide; Chapter 4 is the densest at five instances. 'Not because...but because' appears 37 times across 16 chapters.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md lines 926-1470 (Chapter 4): reduce 'the kind of [noun] that' from 5 instances to at most 1. Keep the strongest or most specific instance; replace the others with direct description that does not use the 'the kind of' template. For the doubled instance at line 1368 ('The kind of notation a person made between cups of coffee. The kind of notation that could destroy a life'), keep one or rewrite both into a single sentence that conveys the same irony without the template. Similarly, reduce 'not because X but because Y' from 3 to at most 1 by varying the explanatory move — e.g., simple declarative, or the unexplained statement that trusts the reader to infer.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 4 (lines 926-1470) must contain: (1) no more than 1 instance of 'the kind of [noun] that'; (2) no more than 1 instance of 'not because... but because' (or 'not because... because'); (3) the remaining instances must be replaced with varied constructions, not simply deleted — the information they carry should still be conveyed.

findings.problem: Prose-formula density: 'not because X but because Y.' Chapter 7 contains 3 instances of this paired-negation construction in only 196 lines (the shortest chapter), making it the densest chapter by ratio. Lines 2479 ('not because it was funny but because it was salesman language') and 2491 ('not because Mort was quaint or meticulous... but because if you sold the wrong machine screw') appear within 12 lines of each other. This construction appears 37 times across 16 of 18 chapters novel-wide. Each instance does the same work: blocking the reader's assumed interpretation and installing a corrected one. At this density, the rhetorical move becomes visible as a tic.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md lines 2441-2636 (Chapter 7): reduce 'not because... but because' from 3 to 1. Keep the strongest instance (line 2491 about the machine screws is the most specific and character-revealing). Replace the other two with varied constructions — direct statement, simple declarative, or an unexplained observation that trusts the reader.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 7 (lines 2441-2636) must contain: (1) no more than 1 instance of 'not because... but because' (or the variant 'not because... because'); (2) the kept instance must be the most specific and least formulaic of the three.

findings.problem: Prose-formula density: 'not because X but because Y.' Chapter 11 contains 4 instances of this construction in only 241 lines — the second-densest chapter by ratio. This chapter already has an overstatement problem (F04); the formula repetition compounds it by making the narrator's post-email processing sound like a refrain rather than genuine thought. Lines 4515 and 4539 are particularly close together, each blocking an interpretation and installing a correction within the same reflective passage.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md lines 4367-4607 (Chapter 11): reduce 'not because... but because' from 4 to at most 1. This chapter's dramatic force comes from the emails themselves, not from the narrator's correction of the reader's interpretation. One corrective move per chapter is sufficient; the others should be replaced with direct statement or removed entirely as part of the overstatement trimming directed in F04.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 11 (lines 4367-4607) must contain: (1) no more than 1 instance of 'not because... but because'; (2) the surviving instance should appear in the chapter's first half (before the email discovery), not in the post-discovery reflection where the narrator is already over-explaining.

findings.problem: Prose-formula density: 'not because X but because Y.' Chapter 13 contains 4 instances of this construction in 366 lines. Lines 5265 and 5295 appear within 30 lines of each other in the hardware store scene. The construction appears 37 times novel-wide across 16 chapters; at this density per chapter, the rhetorical move becomes a predictable narrator habit rather than a deliberate stylistic choice.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md lines 5211-5576 (Chapter 13): reduce 'not because... but because' from 4 to at most 1. Keep the instance at line 5349 ('not because it was heavy but because that was how he'd carried one when he was eight') — it is the most specific and emotionally loaded. Replace lines 5265, 5295, and 5477 with varied constructions.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 13 (lines 5211-5576) must contain: (1) no more than 1 instance of 'not because... but because'; (2) the kept instance should connect to a specific childhood memory or physical detail, not to a generic interpretive correction.

findings.problem: Prose-formula density: 'not because X but because Y.' Chapter 15 contains 4 instances across 705 lines. While less dense by ratio than Chapters 7 or 11, the issue compounds with the 'landed' narrator tic (lines 6655, 6758) in the same chapter, creating a combined prose-formula load of 6 formulaic constructions. Line 6683 ('Not because it was tender. Because it was not. Because it meant the night had not become a movie.') is a strong instance that works; the others are more mechanical.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md lines 6031-6735 (Chapter 15): reduce 'not because... but because' from 4 to at most 2. Keep line 6683 (the strongest). Also address the 'landed' tic at lines 6655 and 6758 — replace at least one of the two uses of 'landed' with a different verb or construction for how language registers on Nate. Target the line 6758 usage ('Now each item landed cleanly'), which is the more mechanical instance.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 15 (lines 6031-6735) must contain: (1) no more than 2 instances of 'not because... but because'; (2) no more than 1 use of 'landed' to describe the impact of language or dialogue on Nate; (3) the kept 'not because' instances must not appear within 100 lines of each other.

findings.problem: Victor's dialogue becomes essay prose. In Chapter 14, Victor delivers multiple compressed aphorisms that read as authorial thesis rather than spoken language: 'Your proposal assumes the paper is the job. It isn't. The paper is the receipt.' (line 5866) and 'They think every bad thing must have one throat you can put your hands around. Mostly it doesn't. Mostly it has six signatures, two favors, a council vote, and a bank that wants what it was promised.' (line 5898). Both are polished to a sheen — balanced antitheses, precisely positioned key words, rhetorical structures designed to land. Victor's established voice (avuncular, indirect, generous with subordinate clauses) can accommodate some polish, but these lines read as composed rather than spoken. When stacked with his Ch16 dialogue, Victor accumulates a disproportionate number of quotable lines that position him as a wisdom-delivery system.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:5866 and 5898: roughen Victor's syntax. The ideas can stay — the novel needs Victor's systemic argument — but the delivery should sound more like a man explaining something to a colleague than an essayist constructing a paragraph. Options: (a) break the balanced structures with hesitation, repetition, or a subordinate clause that wanders before arriving ('The paper is — look, the paper is what's left after the deal happens. It's not the deal.'); (b) have Victor interrupt himself with a practical aside ('You want more coffee? — The paper is the receipt. The deal happened in a room you weren't in.'); (c) let one of the two lines be rough and inarticulate — Victor reaching for an idea he doesn't usually have to explain, finding his usual polish insufficient.
findings.acceptance_test: Lines 5866 and 5898 must: (1) not contain balanced antithetical structures (A is not X. A is Y.); (2) retain Victor's core argument (paper is not the reality, systemic harm has no single throat); (3) include at least one syntactic imperfection — a false start, a subordinate clause that overruns, a practical interruption — that makes Victor sound like a man speaking rather than a writer composing.

findings.problem: Victor's dialogue continues the aphoristic pattern from Chapter 14. At lines 6818-6822: '"This is the part people always think is dramatic," he said. / "It never is. That\'s why it works."' This is a thesis statement about institutional power delivered as dialogue — it reads as a writer's observation, not a boss talking to an employee. Victor's wisdom-dispensing role is now so prominent that his dialogue functions as authorial commentary on the novel's own themes.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:6818-6822: replace Victor's aphorism with dialogue that conveys the same power dynamic (Victor is calm, Nate is trapped, the signing is procedural) through Victor's characteristic indirectness rather than compressed insight. Options: (a) Victor says something practical and deflating ('Sign by the tab. Both copies.'); (b) Victor makes small talk that ignores the gravity of the moment, which is itself the power move ('How's Sarah? She still doing summer school?'); (c) Victor says nothing — the silence is the point. The scene already carries its weight through action.
findings.acceptance_test: Lines 6818-6822 must: (1) not contain a thesis statement about how institutional power works; (2) convey Victor's authority through behavior, tone, or practical speech rather than philosophical observation; (3) sound like something a boss would actually say to an employee in an office, not something a novelist would write about power.

findings.problem: Narrator admiration of dialogue. Line 460: 'It was such an ordinary line, not polished, not delivered for effect. It landed anyway because he could hear the shape of a life under it, the accumulated record of him doing dumb things in this apartment and her observing them without keeping score out loud.' The narrator pauses the scene to explain why Sarah's preceding line was good. This is telling-not-showing at the craft level — the narrator is reviewing the dialogue for the reader instead of letting it register through scene. The word 'landed' here initiates a book-wide tic (15 total uses of 'landed' to describe dialogue impact).
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:460: cut or rewrite the passage. The scene should show Nate's reaction to Sarah's line through action or sensation, not through narrator commentary on the line's quality. Options: (a) replace with a brief physical reaction (Nate's hands stopping, a specific sensory detail of the apartment); (b) cut the entire sentence and let the preceding dialogue speak for itself; (c) keep only the concrete detail ('the accumulated record of him doing dumb things') and cut the framing that calls the line 'ordinary' and says it 'landed.' Also: across the chapter, check for any other uses of 'landed' to describe dialogue impact.
findings.acceptance_test: Line 460 must: (1) not contain the word 'landed'; (2) not contain narrator-level commentary on the quality of a character's dialogue ('ordinary,' 'not polished,' 'not delivered for effect'); (3) show Nate's response to Sarah's line through physical detail or interior sensation, not through craft evaluation.

findings.problem: Two related issues. (1) Narrator admiration at line 2981: 'Nate looked down at his plate again because there was too much grace in seeing his father land a line no one admired on the page, no one framed as charming, no one used to teach the reader anything. He just said it because it fit.' This is the most self-conscious instance of narrator admiration in the manuscript — the narrator explicitly disclaims admiring the line while simultaneously admiring it, and meta-references 'the page' and 'the reader,' breaking the novel's fictional envelope. (2) Overstatement at lines 3113-3115: after Nate's post-diner breakdown lands through physical detail (forehead on steering wheel), the narrator names its own technique: 'That smallness hurt worse than any dramatic version would\'ve.' The scene had already communicated the smallness-that-hurts.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2981: cut the meta-commentary entirely. The passage breaks the fourth wall ('on the page,' 'the reader') in a novel that otherwise maintains close-third integrity. Replace with a brief physical reaction or interior image. At lines 3113-3115: cut 'That smallness hurt worse than any dramatic version would've' — this sentence names the technique the scene is using. Let the scene end on the physical detail of the forehead on the steering wheel.
findings.acceptance_test: Line 2981 must: (1) not contain the words 'page,' 'reader,' or 'charming'; (2) not meta-comment on how a character's line functions as literature. Lines 3113-3115 must: (1) not contain the word 'smallness' or the phrase 'dramatic version'; (2) end the car scene on a concrete image or action.

findings.problem: Two related dialogue-voice issues converge in Chapter 10. (1) 'Do you?' template: the two-word challenge 'Do you?' appears at lines 4071 (Sarah) and 4191 (Ray) within 120 lines. This exact exchange format ('I know.' / 'Do you?') appears 9 times across the novel (also at lines 1004, 1288, 3402, 3470, 4936, 5960, 7652). Three different characters use the identical construction, which should be specific to a single voice if it is to register as character rather than formula. (2) 'Landed' tic: the word appears at lines 4215 ('The word landed and kept landing'), 4766 ('The word landed hard enough that he almost flinched'), and 4862 ('The last part landed harder because it was accurate') — three uses in one chapter, the densest cluster in the novel.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md: (a) Replace the 'Do you?' at line 4071 or 4191 with a challenge specific to that character's voice. Sarah's version could be a pointed silence, a specific counter-question ('Since when?'), or a facial reaction that Nate reads. Ray's version could be more direct ('Bullshit you do.'). Keep at most one 'Do you?' per chapter. (b) Reduce the 'landed' uses from 3 to 1. Keep the strongest (4766 or 4215); replace the others with different verbs for how language registers — 'hit,' 'caught,' or simply cut the narrator's impact commentary and let the dialogue register through Nate's physical or behavioral response.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 10 must: (1) contain no more than 1 instance of the 'Do you?' challenge format; (2) contain no more than 1 use of 'landed' to describe dialogue impact; (3) each character's challenge to Nate must sound distinct from the others — Sarah should not challenge with the same words Ray uses.

findings.problem: Dialogue formality and prose-formula density. Sarah's triple uncontracted 'do not' at line 4884 — 'They do not wake up one day and stop knowing a year of their own life. They do not forget how their own kitchen works. They do not start reacting to their own name like it's a prank.' — uses full formal diction in a moment of acute emotional pressure. The style bible marks Sarah's contraction level as 'high' and specifies that under stress she 'gets very precise, very specific, very calm.' Precision and calm can use contractions; the uncontracted triple 'do not' reads as composed rhetoric rather than pressurized speech. Additionally, Chapter 12 contains 2 instances of 'not because... but because' (approximately lines 5064 and 5106), contributing to the book-wide formula load.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:4884: contract at least two of the three 'do not' forms to 'don't.' Options: keep the first uncontracted for the emphatic opening beat, then contract the second and third ('They don't forget how their own kitchen works. They don't start reacting...'), or contract all three and let the rhetorical force come from the tripled structure and the specific observations rather than from formality. Also: reduce 'not because... but because' from 2 to 0-1 in this chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Line 4884 must: (1) contain no more than one uncontracted 'do not' across the three-sentence sequence; (2) retain the rhetorical triple structure and the specific observations (the year of life, the kitchen, the name-as-prank); (3) sound like Sarah under pressure — precise and devastating, but spoken, not composed.

findings.problem: Two related issues. (1) Narrator admiration at line 1529: 'He laughed harder than the line deserved. Or maybe exactly as hard as it deserved, because what he liked about Sarah was that she said things like that and then went right back to work, trusting the laugh to take care of itself.' The narrator pauses to explain why Sarah's joke about spatulas was good, converting spontaneous laughter into a performance review. (2) Triple-suppression at lines 1827-1829: 'He could have said no. He could have said work, headache, stomach, panic, anything... Instead he said, "Yeah. Sorry. Yeah."' This is a five-beat suppression sequence (the manuscript's longest). The triple-suppression pattern — almost said X, instead said Y — appears 20+ times across 11 chapters; this is the template at its most extended. While the instance works in isolation (it captures Nate's panic before the sex scene), the wider pattern's density means each new instance carries less force.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1529: cut or rewrite. Replace the narrator's explanation of why the laugh was good with a brief physical detail of the laugh itself or its aftermath. Do not explain Sarah's comic rhythm — let it speak for itself. At lines 1827-1829: compress the five-beat suppression to a two-beat. 'He could have said no. Instead he said, "Yeah. Sorry. Yeah."' The intermediate options (work, headache, stomach, panic, anything) are the narrator listing what the reader has already imagined. Trust the reader to know Nate has options.
findings.acceptance_test: Line 1529 must: (1) not contain narrator analysis of Sarah's comic timing or delivery style; (2) not explain why Nate laughed. Lines 1827-1829 must: (1) not enumerate more than one alternative before the 'Instead'; (2) arrive at 'Yeah. Sorry. Yeah.' within two sentences, not five.

findings.problem: Post-Chapter-5 intimacy avoidance. The novel contains one fully rendered sex scene (Chapter 5, lines 1779-1971) that confronts the moral horror of sex under false identity with genuine physical specificity. After that scene, the novel's physical-intimacy dimension disappears entirely for thirteen chapters. Chapter 18, where the couple has survived a crisis and is building something new, mentions 'reaching for her in the dark because she was there and because she reached back' (line 7510) but cuts away. Over an eight-month period of cohabitation, the complete absence of physical intimacy after a single scene creates a pattern of avoidance. The style bible explicitly demands that 'the sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her.' The moral texture of this reckoning changes as the relationship evolves — by Chapter 18, the deception has deepened, not diminished. The absence of any bodily reckoning in the novel's resolution is a gap.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md lines 7476-7510 (the domestic-rhythm passage in Chapter 18): expand the 'reaching for her in the dark' moment at line 7510 into a brief scene (15-30 lines) of physical intimacy that reckons with the new moral landscape. By Chapter 18, Nate is not a stranger performing Dominic — he is someone Sarah has chosen to stay with, and the question is different: what does it mean to touch someone who has decided to accept a version of you that is built on murder? The scene need not be as long or explicit as Chapter 5, but it must be on-page rather than cut-away, and it must engage the body — hands, weight, breath, the physical reality of two people in a bed — not just domestic gesture.
findings.acceptance_test: The passage around line 7510 must: (1) include at least 10 lines of on-page physical contact between Nate and Sarah that goes beyond hand-on-neck or foot-against-calf; (2) register in Nate's interiority the changed moral texture of their physical relationship (he is no longer impersonating Dominic; she is no longer unknowing; the guilt has shifted shape but not disappeared); (3) not cut away before the contact is rendered — no jump to aftermath, no summary.

findings.problem: Dialogue-template recurrence: 'Do you?' The two-word challenge-response 'Do you?' appears at line 3402 (Victor to Nate, during the failed pitch) and line 3470 (Ray to Nate, in the stairwell). Two different characters using the identical construction within the same chapter collapses their voices into one narrator habit. The 'I know.' / 'Do you?' template appears 9 times across the novel (Chapters 4, 9, 10, 12, 14, 18), used by Ray (4x), Sarah (3x), and Victor (1x). A rhetorical challenge this specific should belong to one character's voice, not three.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3402 and 3470: replace one or both with a challenge specific to the speaking character. Victor's version (3402) could be replaced with characteristic indirectness — a question that answers itself ('Then why does your proposal look like you read it for the first time this morning?') or a measured silence that Nate reads as judgment. Ray's version (3470) could be replaced with his more direct, slightly profane register ('Sure you do.' / 'Yeah, right.' / 'That's what you said last time.'). Keep at most one 'Do you?' per chapter.
findings.acceptance_test: Chapter 9 must: (1) contain no more than 1 instance of 'Do you?' as a standalone challenge; (2) Victor's and Ray's challenges must use different words from each other; (3) each challenge must sound like the character delivering it, not like a narrator's all-purpose skepticism tool.
